topic: Structural completeness — does Part 4.6 use the strongest examples? Do counts match tags?
our_argument_summary: Part 4.6 presents three structural findings about the dome's repository: (1) The Monitoring Illusion — most WIN checks are hardcoded or absent, only a handful fetch live data; (2) Relabeling Standard Physics — most WINs rename standard physics mechanisms as 'aetheric'; (3) Post-Hoc Retrodiction — most WINs adopt known observations as predictions. All counts are dynamically computed from per-WIN code_analysis tags at build time.

holes_found.description: Part 4.6 presents only aggregate statistics with zero specific WIN examples. The section discusses '24 hardcoded' and '34 with no validation' but never names a single WIN as illustration. A dome defender could claim the aggregate characterization is misleading because the reader cannot verify any individual case without clicking through all 67 detail blocks. Compare this to other sections (3.5.4, 3.5.5, 3.5.8, 4.5) which cite specific WINs as case studies — Part 4.6 is the only major analytical section that operates entirely at the aggregate level.
holes_found.recommendation: Add 2-3 concrete WIN examples to each subsection. For 4.6.1 (Monitoring Illusion): cite WIN-035 or WIN-039 as examples of 'none' monitoring (appear in WIN list but monitor.py has no code), and WIN-001 or WIN-038 as examples of hardcoded checks (static pred==obs). For 4.6.2 (Relabeling): cite WIN-029 (Schumann → 'aetheric cavity') and WIN-024 (Roaring 40s → 'SAA boundary') which are already discussed elsewhere but not in Part 4.6. For 4.6.3 (Post-Hoc/Derivation): cite WIN-045/046 (tidal periods are known astronomical constants adopted verbatim) and contrast with WIN-044 or WIN-020 (the rare WINs that actually derive from dome geometry, showing what genuine derivation looks like).

holes_found.description: Three WINs remain unreviewed for code_analysis tags: WIN-032 (New Jerusalem pole axis), WIN-033 (Sigma Octantis dimness), WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess). The audit status line says the count 'will update automatically' which is correct, but 3 pending WINs at 95.5% completion means the section's percentages could shift slightly. More importantly, WIN-033 and WIN-065 both rely on the aetheric refraction escape hatch — they are thematically important for the 'derives_from_dome' and 'relabels_standard' tags. Their absence may skew the 'derives_from_dome' count (currently 4/64) slightly.
holes_found.recommendation: Prioritize completing the code_analysis review for WIN-032, WIN-033, and WIN-065. These are not urgent for the prose (the dynamic counts handle it), but completing them brings the audit to 100% and removes the 'audit in progress' caveat.

holes_found.description: CLAUDE.md contains stale code_analysis snapshot numbers from early review: '20/31 hardcoded', '14/31 relabel standard physics', '21/31 post-hoc', 'only 1 derives from dome geometry'. The actual current data is 24/64 hardcoded, 34/64 none, 6/64 live_fetch, 41/64 relabels_standard, 52/64 post_hoc, 4/64 derives_from_dome. While CLAUDE.md is a reference document (not published), the stale numbers could mislead agents and humans who read it for context without checking the live data.
holes_found.recommendation: Update CLAUDE.md's 'Repository Source Code Findings (Part 4.6)' section to either use live-query commands (as the 'Computed Counts' section advises) or update the snapshot numbers to current values. The project's own rule says 'Never write a specific count... when you can instead provide the command to query it live.'

holes_found.description: The 'monitoring: none' category (34/64 = 53%) is now the largest monitoring category, exceeding 'hardcoded' (24/64 = 37.5%). But the prose in Section 4.6.1 leads with the hardcoded pattern and treats 'none' as an afterthought ('Another N have no validation code at all'). The rhetorical emphasis doesn't match the data weight. A dome defender could argue we're burying the lead — over half the WINs have literally zero monitoring code, which is arguably more damning than hardcoded checks.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider restructuring 4.6.1 to lead with the most shocking finding: more than half of the 'confirmed' WINs have no monitoring code whatsoever. Then discuss hardcoded checks as the second tier. The current 'hardcoded → none → live' ordering made sense when hardcoded was the majority, but the data has shifted as more WINs were reviewed.

holes_found.description: The 'derives_from_dome' count (4/64) is extremely low but Part 4.6.3 buries it in a subordinate paragraph: 'Of the N WINs reviewed, only M actually derives its predicted value from the dome's own geometric equations.' This is arguably the single most devastating statistic in the entire section — 94% of 'dome model predictions' are not derived from the dome model — but it reads as a supporting detail rather than a headline finding.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider promoting the 'derives_from_dome' finding to a more prominent position, possibly its own subsection heading or a lead-in to 4.6.3. Something like: 'Of 64 reviewed WINs, only 4 derive their predicted values from the dome's own equations. The other 60 adopt values from standard physics, observational databases, or known constants.' This is the capstone finding that ties all three patterns together.

holes_found.description: Part 4.6 does not cross-reference Part 4.5 (Self-Contradictions). Several self-contradicted WINs also have 'hardcoded' monitoring — meaning the monitoring pipeline is hardcoded to confirm values that the dome's own geometry contradicts. This irony (the automation says 'confirmed' for predictions the model's own math refutes) is not called out anywhere.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence in 4.6.1 or the 'Combined effect' paragraph noting: 'Several of the hardcoded-confirmed WINs are the same ones shown in Part 4.5 to be self-contradicted by the dome's own geometry — the monitoring pipeline reports 'confirmed' for predictions the model's own equations refute.'

stronger_arguments.description: Name the 4 WINs that DO derive from dome geometry (WIN-020, WIN-044, WIN-057, WIN-058) and show what happens when the dome actually uses its own equations. WIN-020 uses gear ratios to reproduce the lunar 18.6-year cycle — but this is curve-fitting gears to a known period, not prediction. WIN-044 derives FSF from V12 geometry. By naming the exceptions, we demonstrate that even the 'genuine' derivations are problematic, strengthening the overall argument.

Create a cross-tabulation showing the overlap between the three patterns. How many WINs exhibit ALL THREE (hardcoded/none + relabels_standard + post_hoc + !derives_from_dome)? If the number is high (likely 30+), state it explicitly: 'N of 64 reviewed WINs exhibit all three patterns simultaneously — they are post-hoc adoptions of standard physics, relabeled with dome vocabulary, and confirmed by monitoring code that cannot fail.' This single sentence is more devastating than three separate subsections.

The shift from 'hardcoded majority' to 'none majority' as more WINs were reviewed is itself noteworthy. The first 31 WINs reviewed (earlier in the project) skewed toward domains that had at least some monitoring code. As the audit expanded to all 67, the proportion with zero monitoring code grew. This suggests the dome's monitoring coverage is even thinner than initial sampling indicated — the WINs with code were the showcase, the rest are empty scaffolding.

kernel_of_truth.description: Part 4.6 is architecturally sound. The decision to compute all counts from per-WIN metadata at build time (rather than hardcoding) is exactly right — it means the section self-corrects as the audit progresses and practices what we preach about not hardcoding statistics. The three-pattern taxonomy (monitoring illusion, relabeling, post-hoc) is genuinely useful and captures real structural problems in the dome's methodology.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Architectural soundness doesn't compensate for the lack of concrete examples. A section that makes strong aggregate claims without naming specific WINs invites the criticism that we're cherry-picking our framing. The fix is additive — the existing structure is good, it just needs illustrative cases.

deeper_analysis:
This holistic review examined whether Part 4.6 properly leverages the curmudgeon's Phase 1 per-WIN findings. The primary gap is that Part 4.6 operates entirely at the aggregate level while the curmudgeon reviews contain rich per-WIN detail that would strengthen the argument. The counts are technically correct (dynamically computed), but the section's persuasive force is reduced by the absence of named examples. A dome defender reading Part 4.6 could say: 'These are just statistics — show me the specific WINs.' Every other major section in our review does show specific WINs. Part 4.6 is the odd one out.

The secondary issue is emphasis: the data has evolved since Part 4.6 was written. 'None' monitoring (53%) now exceeds 'hardcoded' (37.5%), but the prose still leads with hardcoded. The 'derives_from_dome' finding (6% of WINs) is buried in a subordinate clause when it deserves headline treatment.

The CLAUDE.md stale-numbers issue is a process hygiene matter — the published site is correct (dynamic counts), but the project's own reference document contradicts its own rule about not hardcoding counts.

Overall assessment: Part 4.6 is structurally sound and its counts are correct. The improvements needed are rhetorical (add examples, reorder emphasis) rather than factual. No misrepresentation found.

citations_verified: Schumann 1952 derivation referenced in 4.6.2 is historically accurate
95.2% static HTML string claim verified in prior code analysis
citations_unchecked: No external DOIs or URLs appear in Part 4.6 prose — all claims reference internal code analysis
